question: A 69-year-old man with a history of endovascular repair of an abdominal aortic aneurysm 3 years earlier presents with fevers, melena, progressive low back pain, and anemia. Contrast-enhanced CT has been obtained. Which complication of prior endovascular repair best explains this patient's clinical presentation and imaging results?

options.B: Type II endoleak with sac expansion

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image. This is an axial contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showing an endovascular aortic graft (hyperdense metallic appearance) within the expected aortic region. Surrounding the graft/aneurysm sac there is periaortic soft-tissue stranding and multiple small foci of gas/air adjacent to the graft material.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the clinical presentation, which includes fevers, melena, progressive low back pain, and anemia in a patient with prior EVAR. The combination of periaortic inflammatory change with intraluminal/extra-luminal gas around the graft on CT is most consistent with infection of the endograft. The patient’s melena and anemia imply gastrointestinal bleeding, which in this context suggests a communication between the aortic graft/sac and bowel (an aortoenteric fistula).

Then I consider the answer choices in light of the image and presentation. A type II endoleak with sac expansion (B) would produce persistent sac opacification but would not explain surrounding gas or systemic fever. Endograft migration with limb occlusion (C) would present with graft displacement and limb ischemia, not periaortic gas or GI bleeding. A mycotic aneurysm without enteric fistula (D) could explain infection and fever, but would not account for melena/anemia unless there were bowel communication. A retroperitoneal hematoma from rupture (E) would appear as high-attenuation fluid/hematoma and acute hemodynamic features, not periaortic gas and chronic fever.

Finally, tying together the CT evidence of graft-associated gas and inflammation with the patient’s fever and gastrointestinal bleeding, the most coherent diagnosis is an infected endograft with an aortoenteric fistula.
caption: Contrast-enhanced computed tomography scan showing a diffusely thickened aortic wall with inflammatory changes and air foci within the native aneurysm.
sub_caption: Contrast-enhanced computed tomography scan showing a diffusely thickened aortic wall with inflammatory changes and air foci within the native aneurysm.
image_context: A 69-year-old man with a medical history of hypertension, coronary artery disease, and congestive heart failure had presented to an outside facility with worsening low back pain, weight loss, malaise, melena, and fevers. His surgical history was significant for exploratory laparotomy and right hemicolectomy 4 years prior because of numerous tubular adenomas and high-grade dysplasia. He had also undergone infrarenal EVAR 3 years earlier to treat an asymptomatic 6.3-cm abdominal aortic aneurysm. The aneurysm had been repaired using a Gore Excluder endoprosthesis (W. L. Gore & Associates, Flagstaff, AZ) and had required a 32-mm × 45-mm Gore Aortic Extender proximal extension cuff to treat a type Ia endoleak that resolved with deployment. He had been evaluated at an urgent care center for low back pain on multiple occasions in the month preceding his presentation to us. The laboratory results from those visits revealed anemia; however, no further workup had been pursued. An initial computed tomography scan had demonstrated dilated small bowel and a thickened, inflamed aortic wall with air foci within the native aneurysm sac (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig 1</xref>). This finding was concerning for an infected endograft with a possible aortoenteric fistula, and the patient was subsequently transferred to our facility.). This finding was concerning for an infected endograft with a possible aortoenteric fistula, and the patient was subsequently transferred to our facility.Fig 1Contrast-enhanced computed tomography scan showing a diffusely thickened aortic wall with inflammatory changes and air foci within the native aneurysm.
